items.text: Validate your ticket before boarding or risk a €50+ fine — even with a reserved seat.

items.label: Trenitalia
items.text: National rail operator — book at trenitalia.com or Trainline app. Regional trains (Regionale) are cheap; Frecciarossa is the high-speed option between cities.

items.label: Validating
items.text: Insert your paper ticket into the yellow/green stamping machine (obliteratrice) on the platform before boarding. E-tickets don't need validating.

items.label: Classes
items.text: 1st class is worth it on long Frecciarossa routes. Regional trains have no class distinction.

items.label: Strikes (Sciopero)
items.text: Strikes are common. Check trenitalia.com the day before. Core services must run 9–13:00 and 17–21:00 by law.

items.label: Luggage
items.text: No luggage restrictions but overhead racks are small — a 56×45×25cm cabin-sized bag fits most racks.

items.label: Buy before boarding
items.text: Tickets from tabacchi (tobacco shops), newsstands or apps — not usually on the bus. Validate immediately on boarding.

items.label: Rome
items.text: ATAC buses run across the city. Bus 40 and 64 link the train station to the Vatican. Avoid peak hours (8–9am, 6–7pm).

items.label: Milan
items.text: ATM runs an excellent network. Buy 90-minute tickets (€2.20) or a day pass (€7).

items.label: Florence
items.text: ATAC buses and trams. Much of the centre is ZTL (restricted zone) — cars banned, buses and taxis only.

items.label: Naples
items.text: Buses are crowded and slow. The Circumvesuviana train is far better for Pompeii and Sorrento.

items.label: Rome
items.text: Line A (orange) serves Vatican, Spanish Steps, Termini. Line B (blue) serves Colosseum. Single ticket €1.50 — valid 100 minutes.

items.label: Milan
items.text: Best metro in Italy — 5 lines, runs until 12:30am (2am weekends). Integrated with buses and trams.

items.label: Naples
items.text: Limited but useful — Line 1 serves the historic centre, Piazza Garibaldi and the airport.

items.label: No metro
items.text: Florence, Venice and most small cities have no metro. Walking and buses are the way around.
Only use licensed white taxis with a meter — avoid unlicensed drivers at airports and stations.

items.label: Hailing
items.text: Taxis can't be hailed on the street in most cities — find a taxi rank (posteggio) or use the IT Taxi app.

items.label: Fares
items.text: Meter starts at €3–4. Airport surcharges apply. Sunday and night rates (10pm–6am) are higher.

items.label: Tipping
items.text: Not expected but rounding up is appreciated — e.g. pay €20 on a €17.50 fare.

items.label: Apps
items.text: IT Taxi is the official app (works nationwide). Uber operates in Rome and Milan but is pricier.

items.label: What is it?
items.text: The vaporetto is Venice's water bus — the only public transport through the city. Operated by ACTV.

items.label: Tickets
items.text: Single ride €9.50 — expensive. A 24-hour pass (€25) or 48-hour pass (€35) is far better value if you explore.

items.label: Line 1
items.text: Slow boat down the Grand Canal — stops at every landing. Best for sightseeing. Line 2 is express.

items.label: Validate
items.text: Tap your ticket or pass at the yellow reader before stepping onto the pontile (landing stage).

items.label: Water taxis
items.text: Private water taxis are beautiful but cost €80–150 per journey. Split with others to make it worthwhile.
ZTL zones (Zona a Traffico Limitato) are camera-enforced restricted areas in most historic centres — fines arrive months later by post.

items.label: Autostrada
items.text: Toll motorways — pay at booths with cash or card. Fast and well-maintained.

items.label: Driving style
items.text: Italians drive confidently. Lanes are treated as suggestions on country roads. Assertive but calm wins.

items.label: Parking
items.text: Blue lines = paid parking, white = free, yellow = residents only. Parcheggio signs lead to official car parks.

items.label: Speed limits
items.text: 50 km/h in towns, 90 km/h on roads, 110 km/h dual carriageways, 130 km/h autostrada. Strictly enforced by speed cameras.

items.label: Headlights
items.text: Mandatory outside urban areas at all times of day — even in sunshine.
Never use unlicensed taxi touts at airports — they will charge €80–150 for journeys that should cost €25–50. Only use white licensed taxis from the official rank, or pre-book.

items.label: Rome Fiumicino (FCO)
items.text: Leonardo Express train runs every 30 min to Roma Termini — €14, takes 32 min. Much faster and cheaper than a taxi. Also FL1 regional trains to Trastevere, Ostiense and Tiburtina — €8.

items.label: Rome Ciampino (CIA)
items.text: Terravision and SIT buses to Roma Termini — €6, takes 40 min. No train link — bus is the best option.

items.label: Milan Malpensa (MXP)
items.text: Malpensa Express train to Milano Centrale (35 min, €13) or Cadorna (30 min, €13). Malpensa Shuttle bus to Centrale — €10 but slower. Taxi fixed fare: €65 to central Milan.

items.label: Milan Linate (LIN)
items.text: ATM bus 73 to Centrale — €2, takes 25 min. Taxi: €25–30 fixed rate to centre.

items.label: Venice Marco Polo (VCE)
items.text: ACTV water bus (vaporetto) to Venice — €15, scenic, takes 75 min. Alilaguna ferry — similar price. Land taxi to Piazzale Roma — €40, then on foot or water bus. No direct train.

items.label: Florence (FLR)
items.text: Tramvia T2 tram to Santa Maria Novella station — €1.70, takes 20 min. Taxi fixed rate: €22.

items.label: Naples (NAP)
items.text: Alibus to Piazza Garibaldi — €5, takes 30–50 min. Taxi fixed rate: €23 to centre.

items.label: Taxi scam warning
items.text: Always insist the driver uses the meter (tassametro), or agree a fixed price before getting in. Licensed taxis have the city's coat of arms on the door and a meter display.

items.label: Sicily
items.text: Regular ferries from Villa San Giovanni (Calabria) to Messina — 20 min crossing, run by Caronte & Tourist, very frequent. Overnight ferries from Naples to Palermo (10 hrs) and Genoa to Palermo (20 hrs) via Grimaldi and GNV.

items.label: Sardinia
items.text: Ferries from Civitavecchia (near Rome), Genoa, Livorno and Naples. GNV, Tirrenia and Moby Lines operate routes. Crossings take 6–19 hours. Book well ahead in July/August — very busy.

items.label: Amalfi Coast
items.text: Ferry and hydrofoil services connect Amalfi, Positano, Ravello and Salerno from spring to autumn — far more scenic and quicker than the coastal road. ALICOST and TravelMar operate these routes.

items.label: Capri & Ischia
items.text: Hydrofoils (aliscafi) from Naples Beverello pier — 40 min to Capri, 50 min to Ischia. Multiple sailings daily. Slow ferry also available.

items.label: Lake Garda
items.text: Navigarda ferries connect towns including Sirmione, Bardolino, Malcesine and Riva del Garda. Car ferries cross between Torri del Benaco and Maderno. Excellent way to avoid lake road traffic.

items.label: Lake Como
items.text: Regular ferries between Como, Bellagio, Varenna and Menaggio. Car ferries between Bellagio and Varenna. Buy tickets at the dock — no need to book ahead outside peak season.

items.label: Booking tips
items.text: For overnight ferries book 4–6 weeks ahead in summer. A cabin is worth the extra cost for overnight crossings. Bring your own food — on-board restaurants are expensive.

items.label: Roaming (UK)
items.text: Since Brexit, most UK networks charge for EU roaming again. Check your tariff before travel — some (e.g. O2, Three) still include EU data; others charge £2–5/day.

items.label: Buy an Italian SIM
items.text: Cheapest option for longer stays. Available at airports, phone shops and tabacchi. You'll need your passport.

items.label: TIM
items.text: Best rural coverage across Italy, especially in southern Italy and islands. Visitor SIM: ~€15 for 30GB + 200 min.

items.label: Vodafone Italy
items.text: Good coverage, especially in cities and northern Italy. Tourist SIM: ~€15 for 20GB.

items.label: Iliad
items.text: Cheapest option — €9.99/month for 150GB with no contract. Coverage slightly behind TIM/Vodafone in remote areas but excellent in cities.

items.label: WindTre
items.text: Good city coverage, competitive tourist packages. Available at airports.

items.label: eSIM option
items.text: Airalo, Holafly and Ubigi offer Italian eSIMs you can buy before travelling and activate instantly. From around £8 for 5GB. Ideal if your phone supports eSIM.

items.label: Free WiFi
items.text: Most hotels, bars and restaurants offer free WiFi. 'Avete il WiFi?' (Do you have WiFi?) and 'Qual è la password?' (What's the password?) are useful phrases.
